title: Build your own type
instruction:
So far you have used Python's built-in types: strings, ints, lists, dicts.

But what if you need a `User`? A `BankAccount`? A `Product`? These don't exist in Python — you build them.

A **class** is a blueprint for a custom type. It bundles data and the functions that work on that data in one place.

Run this. Then read it — `class User:` defines the blueprint, `User("Alex", 25)` creates an actual user from it.
successMessage: User is your custom type. user is an instance of it — an actual object made from the blueprint. The class defines what all users look like; instances hold specific data.

title: __init__ and self
instruction:
`__init__` is the constructor — it runs automatically the moment you create an instance.

`self` is the instance itself. `self.name = name` stores the name *on that specific instance* (not on the class).

Create a `Product` class with `name`, `price`, and `in_stock` attributes. Then create two products.
starter:
# TODO: define Product class with name, price, in_stock attributes
# Create two Product instances and print their details

codeChecks.message: Store name on self: self.name = name
Store price on self: self.price = price
Create at least one Product instance.
Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: Two products from one class. Each instance has its own independent name, price, and in_stock. The class is the template — instances are the actual things.

title: Methods — things the object can do
instruction:
Methods are functions that belong to a class. They always receive `self` first — Python passes it automatically when you call them.

Add `deposit(amount)` and `get_balance()` to the `BankAccount` class.
starter:
class BankAccount:
    def __init__(self, owner, balance=0):
        self.owner = owner
        self.balance = balance
    
    # TODO: add deposit(self, amount) method
    # TODO: add get_balance(self) method

account = BankAccount("Alex", 1000)
account.deposit(500)
account.deposit(250)
print(account.get_balance())  # 1750

codeChecks.message: Replace the TODO comments with your code.

successMessage: account.deposit(500) reads naturally — the account does the action itself. That is the beauty of methods. The object owns its behaviour.

title: Class variables — shared across all instances
instruction:
**Instance variables** (`self.name`) are unique per object.
**Class variables** are shared across ALL instances — defined directly on the class.

Run this. Notice `species_count` is shared — every new `Animal` increments the same counter.
starter:
class Animal:
    species_count = 0  # class variable — shared
    
    def __init__(self, name, species):
        self.name = name           # instance variable — unique
        self.species = species     # instance variable — unique
        Animal.species_count += 1  # update shared class variable

dog = Animal("Rex", "Dog")
cat = Animal("Whiskers", "Cat")
bird = Animal("Tweety", "Bird")

print(f"Total animals: {Animal.species_count}")
print(f"Dog's name: {dog.name}")
successMessage: species_count is shared — three animals, counter hit 3. instance variables like name are unique per object. Class variables work like global state scoped to the class.

title: __str__ — make print() useful
instruction:
When you `print(object)` without a `__str__` method, you get something like:

`<__main__.Person object at 0x104a5b0>`

Completely useless for debugging. `__str__` tells Python what to show instead.

Add `__str__` to the `Person` class.
starter:
class Person:
    def __init__(self, name, age):
        self.name = name
        self.age = age
    
    # TODO: add __str__ that returns "Person(name, age)"

alice = Person("Alice", 30)
print(alice)  # should show Person(Alice, 30)

codeChecks.message: Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: Now print(alice) gives you something actually useful. Every class should have __str__. You'll learn more dunder methods in Chapter 16.

title: Add validation to methods
instruction:
One of the biggest benefits of OOP: the object can protect its own data.

Add a `withdraw(amount)` method to the bank account. If there aren't enough funds, print an error and don't withdraw.
starter:
class BankAccount:
    def __init__(self, owner, balance=0):
        self.owner = owner
        self.balance = balance
    
    def deposit(self, amount):
        self.balance += amount
    
    # TODO: add withdraw(self, amount) with balance check
    
    def __str__(self):
        return f"{self.owner}: ${self.balance:.2f}"

account = BankAccount("Alex", 100)
account.deposit(50)
account.withdraw(30)
print(account)         # Alex: $120.00
account.withdraw(200)  # should print error

codeChecks.message: Check for insufficient funds before withdrawing.
Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: The account protects itself. No external code can accidentally overdraw it — the validation is baked into the object. This is encapsulation at its most natural.

title: Multiple instances are independent
instruction:
Every instance is a completely separate object with its own data. Changing one doesn't affect another.

Create two bank accounts and verify that deposits to Alice's account have zero effect on Bob's.

codeChecks.message: Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: Two independent objects. Alice's deposit didn't touch Bob's balance. This isolation is a core benefit of OOP — each object manages its own state, no accidental interference.

title: Build a shopping cart
instruction:
Final challenge — no starter code.

Build a `ShoppingCart` class:
- `__init__`: empty items list, total of 0
- `add_item(name, price)`: add to items, update total
- `remove_item(name, price)`: remove from items, update total
- `get_total()`: return total
- `__str__`: return a readable summary

Create a cart, add 3 items, remove 1, print the total.
successMessage: A real shopping cart class! This is the foundation of every e-commerce backend on the planet. Chapter 13 done — you can now create your own custom types in Python.
